speaker: Robert Leffler
rights: University of Baltimore, educational and research use
text: This is December 4th, 1998. This is the stationery right here today to do all history interviews with Bob Laffler, the Negro Leagues in Baltimore. Mr. Laffler wrote a paper on the history of the Black baseball in Baltimore in 1913 and 1950. Mr. Laffler, I wanted to ask you some questions today because the question is about your paper. You could talk about some of the areas of research that you did. I just want to correct one thing. It's an unpublished master's thesis. It was not a paper. It was a big paper, 147 pages. It's one thing. I need to buy my bio. I'm sorry about that. We researched the entire history, season by season, and then we had to go out and in some cases look at the broader. It was almost a mini history of Black baseball for those years. So we had to look at the West Coast and the American Negro National League and independent teams. It was really more than just Baltimore because we had to look at the tenor in which Baltimore played. I guess I should answer them. We're fully in terms of areas. It wasn't like comedy games they won. It was who owned the team? Where did they play? What were some of the issues? Who were some of the players? What were some of the social issues we did a survey about? What was the nature of a black band and white band? What was the nature of a black band? The enterprise was it. It was profitable. What was the nature of the ownership? What was the nature of the ownership? And then also we tried to portray what it was like to be a game. I have a copy of that paper. Can the Bob Bay Bruth Museum have, or they're doing an exhibit? Negro Leaks exhibit? And that's the purpose for which we're doing our research this semester. And after reading through this information this could be very helpful for them. It was background and context. I'd like to talk to Mike about that. I don't want to just reduce that. That's right. Why were you interested in researching the Negro Leaks? It was a first year graduate paper. Originally had Morgan with Thomas Crit as you know as well known. He was a national expert in Blacks in the film. He had been as a kid at the Old Westport Stadium. The map was wrote. He had been played for some of the pro teams in the early 50s of the plate against the U. like Giants. And we were just curious about what we knew about it. But he really liked all the professors you knew. He wanted to get the grants to do the work. And he wanted to find out about it. He literally wanted to find out. So the first paper was the last was the end of the E-lites. Probably the end of the paper. The end of the thesis. And the first paper was the end of the E-lites from the late 40s after the war. It was a 1950s which was their last official year in residence. They played some for historical accuracy purposes. They played some independent games there in 1951. They weren't based here after 1950s. Okay. Could you explain some of the social conditions that existed during the E-lites? Well, obviously. The most prevalent was segregation. And then in public accommodations of plummetly owned stadiums. A lot of the baseball parks were private. So the black teams rented those parks. And there were other bookings. Joe Cambria, maybe Godly, who also owned the Philadelphia 76th of the Abe Saperstein and the Arles Booktries. These guys literally booked them like acts. They were book a whole season like an act. They had a lot of black business, which is one of the things that fascinated the most. They had a lot of things to overcome being taken seriously. And you can read in the Afro-American, in the New York A's, Chicago Defender, that's for a courier, clean call post every day. When you read, you can do, I mean, the gut of this is oral interviews and newspaper. And you can feel these social conditions. And I mean, yeah, I'm not, it is what it is. I mean, they were, they were like all black businesses. One of the side bars of this is I discovered, funeral homes and things like that. I discovered department stores and and some funnest, symphony orchestra that existed in the 20s. There was a black symphony called the A-olean symphony orchestra. The black and musical orchestra. The whole separate society, which comes off of everything the whites had the black symphony. And of course, the last great vestige of it is the quote traditionally African-American colleges. You know, in the University of Texas, the morning has the only thing left. And I mean, yeah, that's not actually that's not true. Sorories, fraternities, separate, like the Baptist Convention and the National Bar Association, the monumental city bar association. These are all sub-secregated medical associations. Not that they're not segregated, but they're a vestige of it. And that was, if you want to look at, you want to look in the mirror for the 40s and the 30s, 20s and the teens. Look at those and you have, you can still sort of see, especially the black colleges, you can see, like baseball. What was different about the Negro legaries and the white groups? Oh, yeah, tremendous. You played sometimes 250 games a year. They played a barnstorm and played lead games. The statistics were very, very sketchy. I mean, they'd root hit 60 home runs. Josh Giddish, someone year, hit eight high home runs. I mean, they would play three, sometimes four games a day. They were almost a bustle. And they played as often as they could play a game. They'd say Chicago and they'd go to the peorian play a semi-protein and pass the hat to make the money. So it was totally different than the Major League Baseball, which was tightly controlled and monitored and owned. Gamling was a problem in Major League Baseball. But a lot of the owners of these teens were, quote, I called them, the commissioners of the illegal lottery. These guys would be perfect. They'd know them all the day since the states are in the lottery business, but in those days the lottery in the black community was very, very much a big deal. And so these quote would go illegal commissioners, if you will. They'll, the word in the black press is sportsman. They call it a sportsman. That means you ran the numbers. I would take it the white league was more profitable. Yeah, but the black teens could be profitable, but the profits were a lot of white and a lot of whites profited from the black teens, booking agents, ballpark owners, etc. etc. There were some ballpark in the bill, Compez Oval, which was the home of the New York Cubans was built in Harlem, Greenfield, which is the home of the greatest team of all. The Pittsburgh profits were built on the hill district and Pittsburgh. But it only was up for six years, but it was a, it had bricks and steel and concrete, just like a regular ballpark. It was torn down just to show you the, you know, the social, the thing was built in the, in the 30s and 32s, torn down at 38 to make way for public housing. There was no, there was no power there. Let's put it that way. When you were doing your research, what, what was the, what didn't you learn and that fascinated you a little bit? I learned a lot of things. I learned, I learned about the separate black society. If I had to put it in a five-man category, separate, unequal, but very, and I have to this day, I think, black history. It's got to spend more time talking about. African-American heritage and pride can be found in the accomplishments of these people, building lives under great unrest, and, and, and building business that's what is impressive to me. I mean, when I taught a street 20 years ago, as always, I've really talked black history roles, talked to civil rights movement, and it's much, much more than that. And it was all, it was, one of the things that killed black baseball was the fact that it was, you know, it wasn't integrated so it was looked down upon, and it was thought it was a negative, black press it, and ended up, I get the same way as you a lot of times. I mean, beating it to death because, you know, the same problem was the disorganization and, you know, the numbers, guys, and all that. But I think the biggest thing was, not necessarily courage, because I think one of the biggest problems, is, being able to value these people, not, with the Mount pedestals, just value, simply value them, as a part of the flip side and white guilt, is it re-end up, you know, putting them on pedestals, and that's also not healthy. Do you think that the real race relations in baseball has improved from the primary release? In baseball, well, baseball is still pretty white, baseball, baseball, hockey, or white basketball, football, and black. There's a lot of black baseball players, but you don't have, like the NFL, I mean, you don't have 80% of the players black, I ain't percent of their gay players play. I guess race relations are, the Tokyo people said it best in 1836, and said about 150, 150 years ago, these people are going to be really mad. This is just not something he recognized after the Americans as people. And he said, you know, these people are going to be very, very upset, and they're going to have a long assimilation period. And it's still going on, and I think that it's a subtle thing. And the cultural strength of the African American group is such that part of what holds it together is emphasizing that culture, which on the flip side, alienates the whites. And so, you don't quite understand why. You take really apt to do a lot of research, and a lot of walking, and somebody else is shoes to understand it, and that's not the offended by it. And that's part of the problem. Did you ever interview any neighborhood players, and if yes, through and what did they say? I mean, when I wrote this, I was 26 years old, so I didn't have them read the history, but I didn't have the aggressiveness that I have today as a business person. I sent requests for an interview to Roy Campanello in New York, and I never heard anything. But I got family members of ownership, but the major guy I interviewed was Clayman Yokeley. And then the other, it was more important for me to interview Dick Powell, who was General Manchardte, and he's still with us, and he's a treasure. And for that, Dick Powell and the same Lacey, the Afro, and Embraidery, I have no feces. But Dick Powell was, I mean, Yokeley, it was a very sad interview because of the Pennsylvania Avenue, it was in 1970, after the riots, but before they did the uptune. And he literally cleared the black historic, the historic black community, all the clubs, the Royal Theater was torn down in 1971. And it was pretty well decimated by the riots, but it's interesting. I had a friend who I talked with, a black friend named Theodore Watkins, who accompanied me to this interview, just quite frankly, in those days, I mean, white, probably the same way today. Yokeley had a shoe shine parlour, and his real sad to see this guy of obvious, athletic bill, 20 years after he pitched him. And this probably his late 50s, low 60s, but not the greatest of health, John the S and the I's, and thinking about that was when Bob Gibson was winning Sy Young's, thinking about this way of that, Bob Gibson did that. I mean, he was that good. And I mean, it was a difficult interview. The thing I remember the most is he had yellow clippings mounted on a poster board in the window of the shoe shine parlour. And still from the 50s, from the Afro. And he because he pitched in the 30s, 40s, 50s. But he was the major guy. He was an important guy. There were, I was more interested in like, Rosster's daughter, the guy the own team, Rosster's restaurant, South Paul, or the Black Sox at one time. And those are kind of people I was interested in, Sam. People that really knew about it. But I probably, if there's a weakness in the thesis, it's interviews. But I also had a very interesting interview with Branch Ricky. But it wasn't, Branch Ricky was dead by the time the thesis was written. It was a happy stance interview. A job I had before college and the company supplied Branch Ricky with oxygen. I didn't even realize I was talking to the real Branch Ricky. I didn't put it in here because I couldn't verify it. It was just by word against theirs. But it was, it was very interesting about what happened. Jackie Robinson in the May of this and all that. Since writing the paper, have you received any updated information back in the days? Oh, I mean, it's, it's an ongoing thing. I mean, I, Dr. Chris wants me to publish it. I still have time. I mean, I have to really clean it up and do what I like to do. And I have to, I mean, I get, I get information all the time. I mean, I, we participated in 1990 in a major conference at the Pratt Library in the spring of 1990. Among the people there was the Pratt Library and Malgood, Monti Urban, Joe Black. Malgood was the first, the late Malgood was the first Black reporter in BC News. In the early 60s, he was a fabulous guy there. He wasn't a participant in the conference, but he overshadowed everybody. He was the same. We gave the best. You'd be climbing the his skin about confidence. And in the same way, he's the same way, but confidence versus the Black. It was, it was, it was really fabulous. But I get stuff all the time. I just, and the thesis is now being, the thesis is now being, he was, I mean, cited in books. There's a number of books. And there's been an explosion. Since I wrote this, there's been an explosion largely on the backs of people like me. This is a pioneering piece. I first delivered the first chapter of this, in Phi Alpha Phi, the history honor society, which I as a member of my days, a thousand. I almost got laughed off the dice because it wasn't thought it was pop culture history. It wasn't people who realized it's the history of people, and some people didn't think of it as important. But it's a pioneering piece. I'm just beginning to realize now, age 53, what it meant. So I, it's an ongoing interest, unfortunately, for me, with my schedule, and what I do, one of this company I don't have time to do what I like today, which would be a bugle of dealt with before. But the thing I'm enjoying is plenty of other people are dealt with, and it would form me, so I get to read it, you know, it's me. Actually, a question. What do you think is a great depression in World War II? What are the impacts of this to, for instance, I'm the neighbor of this? Well, the depression, almost forced baseball out of existence in many cases. You had teams like Philadelphia, Philly, and St. Petersburg, literally in Bankrupt. The Philadelphia Philly, as we're all in marginal operation, playing in an 18,000-seat stadium. I mean, it was built in 1885, and this is in the 30s. We had a lot of the day baseball. That's why night baseball started coming out in 35 with Larry McFail and the Reds, because they just couldn't get people there in a day time. You have 1,000 people, 500 people, and then a ballpark, and a bullet, 30,000. It was like not unusual. So night baseball started coming out. And then in Negro leagues, the depression, like they all say, if a white American gets cold, black American gets pneumonia in bad times. But the Negro leagues, I mean, you didn't have a big infrastructure, you didn't have ballpark, you didn't have mortgages, you know. And they were sort of a page-go-cash operation. So they just, they didn't flourish. World War II, it's ironic that they reached their zenith. They flourished in World War II because you had, it was the beginning of blackson whites being forced together in Liberty Ship, building shipyards like Fairfield, housing projects, especially in the North, especially in Cleveland, and this is my hometown, it's called Chicago, New York, Philly, North of the Mason Dixiline. And to a less degree, they were forced together here, but this is still a segregated city. But they were working together. And I mean, you had negatives like this horrible race ride in Detroit in 1943, which was a result of the forcing, but I mean the forcing was a necessity because of the war. You still had segregated units in the war. But because there was more money, because black employment went up, I mean, they went to games, more people went to games. And the East West game, which is the I annual All Star game, there's an East West, you know, like East, it wasn't necessarily like the West Coast, it was like Chicago and teams were saying Louis, Troy and Cleveland versus like Baltimore, Washington, Pittsburgh, New York, Brooklyn, I mean teams, teams of the East Coast. The East West game, and the interesting thing about the neighborhood, for example, Memphis National also had Furs, you see, Memphis Red Sox, and the Eli Giants went back and forth with the Baltimore National in Columbus, Ohio, I believe or not. But that game was annually held in Chicago and Michigan Park and it threw 50,000 people in 48,000, 50,000, one time that was a writing director to 51,000 set in 1944. So there was more money, and for, I mean, the attendance was the highest, attendance was the highest in profits, Newark, which was played in the Newark Bears, J-group, J-group Field Newark was a casino. And, you know, that's the team that had very low money or the diamond, I mean, that was fabulous to me, and it was probably one of the best run team run by a woman, run by F-A-M, A-M-A, and they's younger, why is it the Queen, they called her the Queen in the New Relies. She was, I think she died in 1988, she was interviewed in the M-E-A, she's in a firm book. Birmingham, Black Barons were at Eastern Team, and you know, they were part of the, that's where William A.'s came from. They were part of the, you know, at least that's why Michael Jordan, when he played in Birmingham, almost wore the BB-A, and not on the Black Barons. So it was, it was just like anything else, they probably wasn't hit as hard in the Great Depression. But I mean, these things were year-to-year things. You know, the organization, the players would jump teams from more money, and the page play, the essential page play here in 1931, and they would have to have a bunch of debts on Pennsylvania, and would get clothing stores and restaurants and laugh after like a third of a season, you know, with the creditors running after them. Do you think that the only, NIG Relieks had an impact on the Civil Rights Movement? Yeah. The only one? Absolutely, because, because the very pressure in NIG Relieks forced the Jack Robinson thing, which forced, and along with World War II, the Civil Rights Movement, somebody had to break the line. And the fact that there was, there were smart baseball people recognized, that there were great players in the NIG Relieks, just like the whole Latin American thing right now. They all have good teams that have laughing scouts, and they break these guys up, just like the cute, they, they, the agent for L. Duke and Hernandez, the Yankees pitcher, getting these guys out of Cuba. That's a great analogy. They get on the boat, they do the boat people thing, and the guy's name is Joe Cubus. That says they, and he's, he holds a trial camp with the greatest players they can get out of Cuba. But what's the same principle? Built back before Pearl Harbor, there's a very great visionary. He wanted to take the St. Louis Browns, which eventually became the Baltimore Royal, boom, the Los Angeles Plain Regal Field, break out, break out the transcontinental thing, at the same time, stock them with the best. This is theory. The real bad team, like the St. Louis Browns, fill it up with fillings, both flirted with the idea of becoming instantly. They knew these players were very, very good, becoming instantly good, because these teams, at the end of the season, in October, November, in the better climate, especially like here, we had an annual nine-game series between the Black Sox or the E-lites, and the major league all-stars, as they were called, bar story. And some of the great players for the white leagues would play it, the Old Westport Park, which is different from Westport State, and it's right down the street, we can, would have been right down the street from the beginning. The Bush and Russes would be, as they would play, at Westport Park, which was the Black State, where Joe Gans bought the late Joe Gans, which was very narrow, over the Cemetery, was one of the early, great Black entrepreneurs in his town, had a hotel on the side of the current post office, at 38. And it's where Morgan played football at one time, at Maryland Baseball Park. But anyway, they knew they were good, the whites knew they were good, and they flirted with the idea of instant success. We'll take the top 10 players in the league, or at least Josh Gibson, you know, central page, this person, that person, and we'll bring him, let me shut this for him. Back in the downstairs. We'll bring him in, we'll be instantly good. And of course, what happened in Backers World War II, and what happened in Jerry Nugin, one of the fillings was, he'd piece a lot of thought about doing it in Doc Crowthrow, his manager, thought about doing it in like 44. But they didn't have the courage or the intellect, well, but that fact, that it's unfair, that Ricky did. And Ricky just could have cared less. Supposedly, I mean, giving Ricky the credit for just being his great visionary, he was practical. He knew, couldn't bring up some of the guys that had been there for a while, like Gibson was too old. And Jack Robinson only played like one year for the Kansas City of Models, but he was a great baseball football, basketball star, could have played pro football at UCLA. So he had the intellect and the hide to handle this. And all that stuff, and he read the books, true, about what the greatest books about, and Ricky getting ready for all of it in salsa. We can't imagine, I mean, we have a segment of our society that's extremely racist on both sides, but especially from a white standpoint. This massive majority of people were like that in those days. They not now, not polite society. But in those days, it was a massive people, especially below the makes of the explainer, extremely big. But Jackie Robinson entered major leagues. What effect did that have on the major leagues? The major leagues itself and the players. Beginning of the year. Because the black media had to be for Jackie. And to be for Jackie, you couldn't be for Jackie. And the coverage in the Afro began to be difficult. The same lacy went on the road with the dogs, with a lot, and the coverage in the Afro and the other papers was less and less, or day to day, I don't know if it was the beginning of the year. And as one might imagine, it's the same thing with black college football. The V-AC, which is the major black conference. There's still a lot of good football players coming out of these black schools. But once Bob Smith went to Michigan State, and Duffy Dorey began, in the 60s, began really recruiting in the South. And then you look at Alabama, and all these other schools, once the line was broken. It's the same thing. Those colleges. They don't have like Morgan struggles to this day. I mean, grandland struggles. I mean, there are some good schools with some good players, but it's not like it was. Did the other players see Jackie Robinson's entrance with the major lead, so there's an opportunity for them? Well, yeah, they all wanted to be in it. I mean, obviously, the summer too old, and then there were quotas. The Dodgers feel that almost no. It was almost like nobody was ever going to go five to four black. The Dodgers were the closest thing to what back a new-gent plan. Because Campanella, when Newcome pitched, Campanella, Robinson, Jim Gilliam, and Duke, when Duke and MacDonald Black pitched that was that. It was four blacks. And Campanella and Robinson were the two big offense. In fact, his new Gilliam would play for the E-Lights also. Gilliam, two, three quarters of the great Dodger Black playing group in the 50s were from here. Joe Black, Jim Gilliam, Roy Campanella, played for the E-Lights. It's a showtime for the E-Lights, Johnson. They were very good things. But yeah, everybody wanted in, and there was a lot of heartache, and there was a lot of sadness, and you know, guys came too late, and there's a lot of very, very sad tales for guys that were like, you know, I think Jackie Robinson's 26 or 27 we got in. There's like guys that were 30-31, and I mean, Gibson's story is really sad, because he gives them a slightly degrades player on a lot of age in the E-Lights. And I mean, Lee on deck, who just got into the Hall of Fame, one 300 games in the E-Lights never got a SNF match with the major E-Lights. So what other things were the downfall in E-Lights? You say Jackie Robinson's entrance into that? Well, then you have everybody, people, the fans don't like it now, because the fans want integrated baseball. So they start discerning, and in the north especially, the Philadelphia stars. When your town gets a black player, now the Phillies, I don't think the Phillies of the A's get a black player until the mid-50s. So pretty much, I think the last team that integrated was 59 with the Red Sox. Pretty much, and Boston was on a big illegal ease in the city. But like, the big Negro League City, New York, Philadelphia, Baltimore, Washington, Pittsburgh, Chicago, Cleveland. I mean, Cleveland, it just killed the fuck guys. The fuck guys won the Negro National League kind of in 1948, you could never play another game. Because in 1948, that's a good example. In 1948, Paige and Dobie both came and were factors in the 48 Indians run to the pen. And then Al Smith came later, I mean, the New Geester, who was at Newark, these players were the gutsy, and it's no point, I mean, the two best teams, day in and day out, even though the Indians could never be the angies. But like, day in and day out, other than the angies were the Dodgers and the Indians, and they were the teams that integrated the emotion. And so, I mean, from a black point of view, I mean, why would you want to go see this team that represented all that made you ashamed in its rickety ballpark somewhere, like in Baltimore? There was still plenty of view of a field. Then they played at Westport Stadium, which was a dump. Literally, it is a dump. Westport Stadium is preserved under tons of land-filled. When they filled the land, they didn't even realize they were covering land markets. It's concrete bleachers are underneath the land-filled behind the aid and super fresh warehouse on an apples road. It was a stock car track. It was all kind of different. Football Stadium for a minor league football. But they were used that for one year. And they played a few games in what was then the beginning of Memorial Stadium, the one-deck Memorial Stadium. They played a couple of games there. But they had to petition the park board and go through a whole lot of stuff. But why would you want to go to one of these places if you could go to the Major League Park, even if you were sitting in a segregated bleachers, you were still watching your guy play Major League Baseball? What do you think of the other significant thing about the NGN? What do you think of the Major League, what do you think of the major generations? I think it's very important for white kids, necessarily black kids, white kids, to understand that blacks were competent in business, and put a lead together under great duress. I think that one of the problems, one of the great, great raises, is for black kids too, there's sort of a backlash in the black community about black companies. I know when I talk to school, I talk mostly black kids after 19. 1973, I talk mostly black schools. And the thing I would all hear of black doctors, black lawyers, one time I had a law class and we went to a carwhackies court, and there were two black lawyers representing this one guy who was probably guilty as the kid that kids knew about this guy. But there's one very massive lawyer named McAllister who's no longer with us without the pair of case and the rest of the case, and the kids were just raised afterward and went back to the next day. And it's always like that. And so there's a racist attitude among black kids about black professionals. It's not fair. So they need to realize a confidence level and to defeat this negative. And white kids can't even conceive of it. And I think that's what's important, because the problem with black history, the way it's presented now, becomes very happenable. It needs to be presented in that way. You have to study this. It needs to be presented in a natural way, a natural, forthright way to pay. And white kids can build America. Blacks can build America. Everybody can build America. So when the black guys come out, it'll like to say, you know, we build America. You know. It should make it into a curriculum somewhere. I mean, it would be something that kids would get really a lot of fun. Other than Jackie Robinson broke the column line. I mean, it needs to be structure, players, leagues, teams, enterprise, stadium, or bookings, all the stories, barnstorming across the South, secondary teams. I mean, there was a doctor that owned the team in Edgewater that once played a barnstorming game against the Baltimore County Turner Station, where Kweisy and Fumig grew up. And once played a barnstorming team, a barnstorming game against the famous Chicago-American Giants, a patch of grass that's still there over next to the right behind the old Western Electric plant. And he's Baltimore. They were probably Edgewater Giants. I met the guy's son a few years ago. He would read my thesis there. That's in my thesis. I mean, these are all the stories that things that happen. And people realize that the same things happen a certain way. Then they begin to loosen up on some. And let go of some of the pressures that they hold. That's why I think it's important. Well, thank you for taking the time to talk to me today. No problem. I will be in touch with the heavy, but the questions. Okay. Thank you.

Negro Leaks exhibit?

I don't want to just reduce that.

But he really liked all the professors you knew.
He wanted to get the grants to do the work.
And he wanted to find out about it.
He literally wanted to find out.
So the first paper was the last was the end of the E-lites.
Probably the end of the paper.
The end of the thesis.
And the first paper was the end of the E-lites from the late 40s after the war.
It was a 1950s which was their last official year in residence.
They played some for historical accuracy purposes.
They played some independent games there in 1951.
